data.epi_content:
She saw it.

The woman she thought was her friend, with a strange expression of tears in her eyes yet a smile on her lips, as her head separated from her body.

Where should I begin this story?

Vivian, a beautiful girl with talent in magic, intelligent but naive despite her smarts, entered the Academy.

Despite her excellent talents, without the sponsorship of her late father's friend, entering the Academy would have been impossible after her once-proud father passed away.

Well, that's not really an important story.

It's not particularly exciting, just the enviable tale of someone else that makes one feel bitter.

So where should I begin to make this story interesting?

Should I start from the day I was slapped by that pale, gloomy-looking lady?

To explain simply, the three of them were friends.

When she first entered the Academy, those two were the first peers she could call friends.

But as time passed, their relationships began to crack.

Anyone could see this was the natural progression, but the parties involved couldn't see it themselves.

To put it somewhat harshly:

One was a pretty-faced simpleton who had never properly made friends before.

One was a nobody who locked herself in a greenhouse and had never accomplished anything on her own.

And the remaining one was a foolish gentleman overflowing with knowledge but severely lacking in wisdom.

In other words, all three were immature, as most children are.

And immaturity in relationships often leads to bad endings.

Vivian vaguely knew that Evan had developed feelings for her.

She wasn't certain, but she could sense that Evan's affection toward her wasn't the same kind of friendly fondness he showed to Erica, but rather that distinctive, clingy emotion that comes with romantic interest.

And Erica, who liked Evan, knew this too.

So, Erica began to dislike Vivian.

Vivian had subtly called Evan aside and rejected his feelings, but if the problem could have been solved so easily, it would have been simple.

The human heart is complex yet simple in other ways, easily becoming entangled.

Erica began pushing Vivian away, and Vivian simply disliked that.

Erica grew to hate Vivian so much that she began intimidating her in ways that might seem cute from an adult's perspective.

Being a well-regarded noble, she had quite a following.

She would speak disrespectfully, show some anger, and even try to utter clumsy insults.

Though she failed because she couldn't bring herself to use vulgar language.

The more this happened, the more exhausted they both became.

The difference was that as Vivian grew tired, she tried desperately to hold onto Erica, her only female friend, while Erica gave up on separating Vivian from Evan and just tried to distance herself from both of them.

Erica pushed Vivian away, and Vivian pursued her despite being pushed away.

The day she was slapped was similar.

Leaving behind the girls she usually hung out with, Erica seriously made a request.

Please stay away from Evan, or better yet, stay out of her sight.

She asked why Vivian kept seeking her out when they were from different classes—was it to torment her?

Vivian didn't give any answer, or more precisely, couldn't.

She didn't know what to say.

Nevertheless, after hearing those words filled with frustration and slight despair, Vivian sensed it.

If she didn't stop Erica from leaving now, their relationship would be severed for good.

And now, she begins to wonder if she should have avoided Erica instead.
data.epi_content2:
Since that day, even Vivian felt something had become twisted.

From then on, everything seemed to go wrong.

More precisely, not Vivian, but Erica.

While Vivian herself was fine, Erica seemed to break apart whenever she approached her.

After being slapped, Vivian cried for a long time, feeling somewhat humiliated and thinking she had lost a friend, only to coincidentally meet Evan and pour out her feelings.

That day, she heard that Evan and Erica had fought, exchanging harsh words.

Upon hearing this, Vivian clutched her head, and with complicated feelings, drew a warm bath and soaked in it for a long time.

She didn't voice her inner thoughts.

She just kept them inside, letting them ferment.

To her, Evan as a man wasn't very important.

But Evan as a friend was quite important.

To her, Erica as a woman wasn't very important.

But Erica as a friend was very important.

The same applied to others.

If only Erica had met her end in a quieter way, a death that would have been easily forgotten in her mind.

If only she hadn't met such a dramatic end.

She would have been forgotten, and Vivian, after quickly shaking off a bit of sadness, would have gone on to live a life gradually learning about love.

But as everyone knows, the result was that she committed an extreme act and met a spectacular end.

Because of this, she wonders.

What if she had held onto Erica then?

What if she hadn't left the room when told to?

What if she had offered comfort instead of anger when Erica pointed a gun at her head and fired, whether it was a joke or serious?

Would something have been different?

She didn't think at all about the people who died because of Erica.

They were nameless people she wasn't close to and had no desire to befriend.

After all, why would Vivian sympathize with them?

They were probably the kind of people who locked Erica in a small locker and laughed gleefully.

They were likely the culprits who drove the once confident and proud Erica to attempt to blow her own head off in such a haggard state.

In Vivian's mind, they were no better than beasts.

Therefore, they probably deserved to die.

There was no way to save Erica.

Even when Vivian sought out the person with the greatest power she knew, kneeling and begging, it was of no use.

The day before the trial.

When Erica was still confined in the underground prison before being brought here, Vivian went to see the Crown Prince.

After his class, as usual, he was walking to a bench, putting a pipe in his mouth and smoking tobacco.

"Your Highness Johannes, I have a favor to ask."

"I told you that you could drop the 'Your Highness.'

Since this is your first request, I'll grant it if possible. Tell me, Vivian."

"...Can you save Erica?"

At those words, the Crown Prince snorted.

"I said 'if possible.'
data.epi_content3:
Go back, Vivian. Neither you nor I can do anything about this."

Then he put the pipe back in his mouth and started looking somewhere.

In other words, he just stared blankly into space as if recalling something.

Vivian stood there for a long time, staring at the Crown Prince, before speaking again.

"Then, can you at least let me meet Erica?"

Erica was imprisoned in the underground dungeon that had long been unused, originally built when the Academy was first established to hold rebellious noble children.

At those words, the Crown Prince removed the pipe from his mouth and crossed his legs.

"As long as you don't do anything foolish.

I know very well that with your magic, you could cut through the bars, slaughter the guards, and release her into the Academy."

"That won't happen. I just want to meet—"

"Take her there."

The Crown Prince interrupted Vivian.

Then, a human figure emerged from his shadow and guided Vivian.

And when she visited, instead of resolving the knot inside her, she heard the words she least wanted to hear from Erica.

That she envied her, was jealous of her.

That's why she hated her so much.

She said such things with a smile as if they were nothing.

She might have begged for her life, saying she was about to die.

She could have reproached her, saying that with her magic, couldn't she get her out of there?

If only it had been something like that.

Instead, she just heard that Erica envied her, and then left.

That night, Vivian couldn't sleep.

It felt like Erica had become the same as those other women who disliked her...

And the next day, just like when she was slapped by Erica, she unconsciously poured out her emotions.

Just like then, with Evan by her side.

The intense emotions that welled up were not something a girl who hadn't yet become an adult could suppress.

If that were possible, it would mean her emotions had already been worn down.

The difference was that Evan was also in a daze.

"...Evan, what should we do about Erica...?"

"I don't kn—"

-KILL HER!

-KILL HER!

-KILL HER!

Evan and Vivian stood close together, talking.

But there was no sign of tender feelings or, conversely, discomfort about such emotions.

They were essentially at a funeral.

When Evan tried to answer Vivian's question, his words were drowned out by the crowd's chanting.

Vivian and Evan trembled, contemplating what was to come.
